1.description: Unusual weather damages, P&C market share, and P&C exposure across states. These figures present the state-level mean (on the left side) and median (on the right side) of variables related to the instrumental variable. Panel A summarizes state-quarter unusual weather damages in $million. Panel B summarizes state-group-year P&C market share in percentages. To obtain a group's market shares, I first calculate individual P&C divisions' market share using their direct premiums written as a share of all P&C insurers operating in a state-year. I then sum all of the market shares for each state across the divisions within a group. Panel C summarizes the products between state-quarter unusual weather damages and state-group-year market share, at state-group-quarter level in $000. Panels B and C only use insurance groups in my pricing sample that contain P&C divisions. TableIA.XIIin theInternet Appendixreports summary statistics for each state.

2.description: Life insurance premiums and affiliated P&C losses. These figures present binned scatter plots of demeaned life insurance premiums against demeaned affiliated P&C losses, using life insurers with P&C affiliates. Life insurer-product-month-level premiums in quarterqare matched to losses by the P&C divisions within the same group inq−4toq−1. I demean life divisions' policy premiums and their P&C affiliates' reported and instrumentedP&CLossby subtracting insurer-product and product-month averages. Instrumented losses are predicted P&C losses from the first stage of the instrumental variable regression, corresponding to column (1) in TableIII, Panels B and D. The horizontal axis is the demeaned reported P&C losses in the figures on the left and the demeaned instrumented P&C losses on the right. Panel A corresponds to permanent life policies. Panel B corresponds to 10-year term policies. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Life insurance premiums before/after instrumented P&C losses. These figures show the relationship betweenP&CLossin any four consecutive quarters instrumented by unusual weather exposure during the same period and life divisions' premiums in each of the six months before and 18 months after the loss. The graphs plot the second-stage instrumental variable estimate of the coefficientβtand the associated 95% confidence interval using the regressionLifePremiumi,p,t=βt·P&CLossg,(qtoq+3)+FEi,p+FEp,t+εi,p,t, wheretcan be any of the six months immediately preceding quarterqor any of the 18 months immediately following quarterq+3. The dependent variable is the dollar premium for productpoffered by life insureriin montht. Life insureribelongs to groupg. I setP&CLossfor life insurers without P&C affiliates to zero. The instrument forP&CLossg,(qtoq+3),P&CUnusualWeatherExposureg,(qtoq+3), is constructed as detailed in SectionIII.B.2.FEi,pare insurer-product fixed effects.FEp,tare year-month-product fixed effects. Standard errors are corrected for clustering at the group-quarter level (for standalone firms, at the individual insurer-quarter level). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Internal financial transfers and affiliated P&C losses. These figures present the relationship between internal financial transfers and P&C divisions' losses using binned scatter plots. Panel A uses transfers made by life divisions to the rest of the group. Life divisions' total transfers are in yeary, as a percentage of the life division's capital in yeary−2. These transfers are matched to P&C affiliates' losses (both reported and instrumented) iny−1scaled by lagged P&C assets iny−2. I demean life divisions' transfers, and reported and instrumentedP&CLossby subtracting division and year averages. Instrumented losses are predicted with the regression in column (3) of TableVI, Panel A. Panel B uses transfers made to P&C divisions from the rest of the group. P&C divisions' total transfers received are in yeary, as a percentage of the P&C division's capital in yeary−2. These transfers are matched to the P&C division's own losses (both reported and instrumented) iny−1scaled by lagged assets iny−2. I demean P&C divisions' transfers, and reported and instrumentedP&CLoss, by subtracting division and year averages. Instrumented losses are predicted by regressing losses on the instrument with division and year fixed effects. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
